question_ar: مَا حُكْمُ الفِعْلِ المُضَارِعِ إِذَا اتَّصَلَتْ بِهِ نُونُ التَّوْكِيدِ مُبَاشَرَةً، كَمَا فِي «لَيُسْجَنَنَّ»؟
question_en: What is the iʿrāb status of a present verb when the heavy nūn of emphasis attaches to it directly, as in «لَيُسْجَنَنَّ»?
expected_answer: مَبْنِيٌّ عَلَى الفَتْحِ — it is built (mabnī) on fatḥ.
expected_reasoning: The present verb is normally muʿrab, but it is BUILT in exactly two cases: when the heavy/light nūn of emphasis attaches directly (→ mabnī on fatḥ) and when the nūn of the feminine plural attaches (→ mabnī on sukūn). «يُسْجَنَنَّ» has the direct nūn of emphasis, so it is mabnī ʿalā al-fatḥ, not marfūʿ.

question_ar: فِي «النِّسَاءُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلَادَهُنَّ»، هَلِ الفِعْلُ «يُرْضِعْنَ» مُعْرَبٌ أَمْ مَبْنِيٌّ، وَعَلَامَ؟
question_en: In «النِّسَاءُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلَادَهُنَّ», is the verb «يُرْضِعْنَ» muʿrab or mabnī, and on what?
expected_answer: مَبْنِيٌّ عَلَى السُّكُونِ لِاتِّصَالِهِ بِنُونِ النِّسْوَةِ — built on sukūn because the nūn of the feminine plural is attached.
expected_reasoning: The present verb is built (not declined) when the nūn al-niswah attaches; its ending is mabnī ʿalā al-sukūn and the nūn is a subject pronoun in raf‘. Treating it as marfū‘ by ḍamma would be the classic error the building rule guards against.

question_ar: مَا عَلَامَةُ نَصْبِ جَمْعِ المُؤَنَّثِ السَّالِمِ، كَمَا فِي «إِنَّ المُسْلِمَاتِ صَابِرَاتٌ»؟
question_en: What is the sign of naṣb for the sound feminine plural, as in «إِنَّ المُسْلِمَاتِ صَابِرَاتٌ»?
expected_answer: يُنْصَبُ بِالكَسْرَةِ نِيَابَةً عَنِ الفَتْحَةِ — it takes kasra in place of fatḥa.
expected_reasoning: The sound feminine plural is one of the nouns that deviate from the original signs: it is naṣb by kasra (not fatḥa). «المُسْلِمَاتِ» is the ism of إِنَّ in naṣb, shown by kasra. Reading the kasra as a jarr sign would misassign its function.

question_ar: بِمَ تُرْفَعُ الأَسْمَاءُ الخَمْسَةُ، وَمَثِّلْ بِكَلِمَةِ «أَبٌ» فِي مَوْضِعِ رَفْعٍ؟
question_en: By what are the Five Nouns put in raf‘, and illustrate with «أب» in a raf‘ position?
expected_answer: تُرْفَعُ بِالوَاوِ، نَحْوَ: «جَاءَ أَبُوكَ» — they take wāw, e.g. «جَاءَ أَبُوكَ».
expected_reasoning: The Five Nouns (أَبٌ، أَخٌ، حَمٌ، فُو، ذُو) take letters as signs: wāw for raf‘, alif for naṣb, yā’ for jarr, on condition they are singular, in genitive construct, and not annexed to the speaker's yā’. «أَبُوكَ» shows raf‘ by wāw as fā‘il.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ كَلِمَةَ «مُعَلِّمِينَ» فِي «رَأَيْتُ مُعَلِّمِينَ مُجْتَهِدِينَ».
question_en: Parse «مُعَلِّمِينَ» in «رَأَيْتُ مُعَلِّمِينَ مُجْتَهِدِينَ».
expected_answer: مَفْعُولٌ بِهِ مَنْصُوبٌ، وَعَلَامَةُ نَصْبِهِ اليَاءُ لِأَنَّهُ جَمْعُ مُذَكَّرٍ سَالِمٌ — direct object in naṣb, sign is yā’ (sound masculine plural).
expected_reasoning: The sound masculine plural is naṣb and jarr by yā’, raf‘ by wāw. «مُعَلِّمِينَ» is the object of رَأَيْتُ, so naṣb, and the yā’ is its naṣb sign — not a jarr sign. «مُجْتَهِدِينَ» follows as naʿt, agreeing in case by the same yā’.

question_ar: مَا المُبْتَدَأُ وَمَا الخَبَرُ فِي «العِلْمُ نُورٌ»، وَمَا حُكْمُهُمَا الإِعْرَابِيُّ؟
question_en: Identify the mubtadaʾ and khabar in «العِلْمُ نُورٌ», and their iʿrāb.
expected_answer: «العِلْمُ» مُبْتَدَأٌ مَرْفُوعٌ، وَ«نُورٌ» خَبَرٌ مَرْفُوعٌ — both marfū‘.
expected_reasoning: A nominal sentence's mubtadaʾ and khabar are both raf‘; the copula 'is' is implied, not lexical. «العِلْمُ» raf‘ by ḍamma as mubtadaʾ, «نُورٌ» raf‘ by ḍamma as khabar completing the meaning. No verb is sought.

question_ar: فِي «فِي الدَّارِ رَجُلٌ»، أَيْنَ الخَبَرُ، وَلِمَ تَقَدَّمَ، وَمَا إِعْرَابُ «رَجُلٌ»؟
question_en: In «فِي الدَّارِ رَجُلٌ», where is the khabar, why is it fronted, and how is «رَجُلٌ» parsed?
expected_answer: الخَبَرُ شِبْهُ الجُمْلَةِ «فِي الدَّارِ» مُتَقَدِّمٌ، وَ«رَجُلٌ» مُبْتَدَأٌ مُؤَخَّرٌ مَرْفُوعٌ — the prepositional phrase is the fronted khabar; «رَجُلٌ» is the delayed mubtadaʾ.
expected_reasoning: When the mubtadaʾ is indefinite and the khabar is a prepositional/adverbial phrase, the khabar is obligatorily fronted to avoid beginning with a bare indefinite. «فِي الدَّارِ» is khabar muqaddam (its metaphorical link to a hidden 'present/exists'); «رَجُلٌ» is mubtadaʾ muʾakhkhar, raf‘ by ḍamma. Reading «رَجُلٌ» as fā‘il of a deleted verb is the wrong analysis under naḥw of the nominal sentence.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «الجَوُّ» وَ«بَارِدًا» فِي «كَانَ الجَوُّ بَارِدًا».
question_en: Parse «الجَوُّ» and «بَارِدًا» in «كَانَ الجَوُّ بَارِدًا».
expected_answer: «الجَوُّ» اسْمُ كَانَ مَرْفُوعٌ، وَ«بَارِدًا» خَبَرُ كَانَ مَنْصُوبٌ — ism kāna marfū‘, khabar kāna manṣūb.
expected_reasoning: كَانَ and its sisters raise the mubtadaʾ as their ism and put the khabar in naṣb. «الجَوُّ» raf‘ by ḍamma (ism kāna), «بَارِدًا» naṣb by fatḥa (khabar kāna). This inverts the bare nominal sentence where both would be marfū‘ — the governing verb decides the case.

question_ar: كَيْفَ تُمَيِّزُ «كَانَ» النَّاقِصَةَ مِنَ «كَانَ» التَّامَّةِ؟ مَثِّلْ بِجُمْلَتَيْنِ مَضْبُوطَتَيْنِ.
question_en: How do you distinguish defective كَانَ from complete كَانَ? Give two vocalized examples.
expected_answer: النَّاقِصَةُ تَحْتَاجُ خَبَرًا مَنْصُوبًا («كَانَ زَيْدٌ قَائِمًا»)، وَالتَّامَّةُ تَكْتَفِي بِمَرْفُوعٍ بِمَعْنَى «وُجِدَ/حَدَثَ» («إِذَا كَانَ الشِّتَاءُ»).
expected_reasoning: Defective كَانَ does not give a complete meaning by its marfū‘ alone — it needs a manṣūb khabar; «قَائِمًا» is that khabar. Complete كَانَ means 'existed/occurred' and is satisfied by its fā‘il with no khabar; «الشِّتَاءُ» is its fā‘il marfū‘. Misreading the complete one as defective would invent a phantom khabar and mis-case the following word.

question_ar: كَمْ مَفْعُولًا تَنْصِبُ «ظَنَّ»، وَأَعْرِبْ مَفْعُولَيْهَا فِي «ظَنَنْتُ الخَبَرَ صَادِقًا».
question_en: How many objects does «ظَنَّ» take, and parse them in «ظَنَنْتُ الخَبَرَ صَادِقًا».
expected_answer: تَنْصِبُ مَفْعُولَيْنِ: «الخَبَرَ» مَفْعُولٌ بِهِ أَوَّلُ مَنْصُوبٌ، «صَادِقًا» مَفْعُولٌ بِهِ ثَانٍ مَنْصُوبٌ — two objects, both manṣūb.
expected_reasoning: ẓanna and its sisters (verbs of the heart) enter a nominal sentence and put BOTH the original mubtadaʾ and khabar in naṣb as two objects. «الخَبَرَ» is mafʿūl awwal, «صَادِقًا» mafʿūl thānī — originally mubtadaʾ + khabar. Treating «صَادِقًا» as a ḥāl would miss that ẓanna's two objects are the heart of the construction.

question_ar: مَا الفَرْقُ الإِعْرَابِيُّ بَيْنَ «عَلِمْتُ زَيْدًا قَائِمًا» وَ«عَلِمْتُ المَسْأَلَةَ»؟
question_en: What is the iʿrāb difference between «عَلِمْتُ زَيْدًا قَائِمًا» and «عَلِمْتُ المَسْأَلَةَ»?
expected_answer: الأُولَى «عَلِمَ» قَلْبِيَّةٌ تَنْصِبُ مَفْعُولَيْنِ، وَالثَّانِيَةُ «عَلِمَ» بِمَعْنَى عَرَفَ تَنْصِبُ مَفْعُولًا وَاحِدًا.
expected_reasoning: عَلِمَ is a sister of ẓanna (two objects) when it means yaqīn/conviction about a nisbah: «زَيْدًا» + «قَائِمًا» are both manṣūb objects. But عَلِمَ meaning 'to recognize/know' a single thing takes one object: «المَسْأَلَةَ» alone. The verb's sense decides the number of objects — a surface-only reading cannot tell them apart, so this is reasoning-dependent.

question_ar: مَا إِعْرَابُ «رَغْبَةً» فِي «اجْتَهَدْتُ رَغْبَةً فِي النَّجَاحِ»؟
question_en: Parse «رَغْبَةً» in «اجْتَهَدْتُ رَغْبَةً فِي النَّجَاحِ».
expected_answer: مَفْعُولٌ لِأَجْلِهِ مَنْصُوبٌ — maf‘ūl li-ajlih in naṣb.
expected_reasoning: The maf‘ūl li-ajlih is a verbal noun stating the REASON for the action, sharing the main verb's time and doer. «رَغْبَةً» is a maṣdar qalbī giving the motive of اجْتَهَدْتُ, hence naṣb by fatḥa. It is not a mafʿūl bih nor a ḥāl, because it answers 'why', not 'what' or 'in what state'.

question_ar: تِلْمِيذٌ أَعْرَبَ «حُبَّ» فِي «زُرْتُكَ حُبًّا لَكَ» مَفْعُولًا بِهِ. قَوِّمْ إِعْرَابَهُ.
question_en: A student parsed «حُبًّا» in «زُرْتُكَ حُبًّا لَكَ» as a direct object. Evaluate.
expected_answer: الإِعْرَابُ خَطَأٌ؛ الصَّوَابُ أَنَّهُ مَفْعُولٌ لِأَجْلِهِ مَنْصُوبٌ.
expected_reasoning: A mafʿūl bih is what the action falls upon; here زُرْتُكَ already has its object in the كَ. «حُبًّا» is a maṣdar qalbī expressing the MOTIVE for visiting ('out of love for you'), matching the verb's doer and time — the precise definition of maf‘ūl li-ajlih. Calling it a mafʿūl bih confuses 'what' with 'why' and double-assigns objecthood.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «تَكْرِيمًا» فِي «كَرَّمْتُ الضَّيْفَ تَكْرِيمًا».
question_en: Parse «تَكْرِيمًا» in «كَرَّمْتُ الضَّيْفَ تَكْرِيمًا».
expected_answer: مَفْعُولٌ مُطْلَقٌ مَنْصُوبٌ مُؤَكِّدٌ لِعَامِلِهِ — absolute object in naṣb, confirming its verb.
expected_reasoning: The maf‘ūl muṭlaq is a maṣdar from the verb's own root, in naṣb, that confirms, specifies the type, or counts the action. «تَكْرِيمًا» is the maṣdar of كَرَّمَ and confirms it (taʾkīd). It is not a maf‘ūl li-ajlih (no 'why' motive) and not a mafʿūl bih (the object is «الضَّيْفَ»).

question_ar: بَيِّنْ نَوْعَ المَفْعُولِ المُطْلَقِ فِي «سَعَيْتُ سَعْيَ المُجِدِّ»، وَمَا فَائِدَتُهُ؟
question_en: State the type of maf‘ūl muṭlaq in «سَعَيْتُ سَعْيَ المُجِدِّ» and its function.
expected_answer: مَفْعُولٌ مُطْلَقٌ مُبَيِّنٌ لِلنَّوْعِ، مَنْصُوبٌ، وَهُوَ مُضَافٌ — type-specifying absolute object, manṣūb, and it is a muḍāf.
expected_reasoning: A maf‘ūl muṭlaq specifies TYPE when the maṣdar is qualified by an adjective or, as here, by iḍāfa: «سَعْيَ المُجِدِّ» = 'the striving of a diligent person', describing the manner of the action. «سَعْيَ» is manṣūb maf‘ūl muṭlaq and muḍāf; «المُجِدِّ» is muḍāf ilayh majrūr. The iḍāfa is what converts mere confirmation into type-specification.

question_ar: مَا إِعْرَابُ اسْمِ «لَا» النَّافِيَةِ لِلْجِنْسِ فِي «لَا طَالِبَ مُهْمِلٌ»؟
question_en: What is the iʿrāb of the noun of «لا» of absolute negation in «لَا طَالِبَ مُهْمِلٌ»?
expected_answer: اسْمُ «لَا» مَبْنِيٌّ عَلَى الفَتْحِ فِي مَحَلِّ نَصْبٍ — its noun is built on fatḥ, in the place of naṣb.
expected_reasoning: لَا of absolute negation (li-nafy al-jins) works like إِنَّ but builds its single, immediately-following, indefinite noun on what would be its naṣb sign. «طَالِبَ» is mabnī ʿalā al-fatḥ fī maḥall naṣb (ism lā); «مُهْمِلٌ» is its khabar marfū‘. Reading «طَالِبَ» as manṣūb-by-fatḥa misses that it is built, not declined.

question_ar: مَتَى يَجِبُ إِهْمَالُ «لَا» النَّافِيَةِ لِلْجِنْسِ؟ مَثِّلْ، وَبَيِّنِ الإِعْرَابَ.
question_en: When must «لا» of absolute negation be cancelled (muhmalah)? Illustrate and parse.
expected_answer: تُهْمَلُ وَيَجِبُ تَكْرَارُهَا إِذَا دَخَلَتْ عَلَى مَعْرِفَةٍ أَوْ فُصِلَتْ عَنِ اسْمِهَا، نَحْوَ «لَا زَيْدٌ عِنْدَنَا وَلَا عَمْرٌو».
expected_reasoning: لا li-nafy al-jins requires its noun to be indefinite and adjacent. If it governs a definite noun, or a separator intervenes, it is cancelled and repeated; its noun then returns to being a mubtadaʾ marfū‘. «زَيْدٌ» is a proper (definite) noun, so لا is muhmalah; «زَيْدٌ» is mubtadaʾ marfū‘, with لا repeated. Applying the genus-negation building here would be a category error.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «خَالِدًا» فِي «حَضَرَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَّا خَالِدًا».
question_en: Parse «خَالِدًا» in «حَضَرَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَّا خَالِدًا».
expected_answer: مُسْتَثْنًى بِإِلَّا مَنْصُوبٌ — excepted noun after إِلَّا in naṣb.
expected_reasoning: In a positive, complete (tāmm mūjab) exception, the noun after إِلَّا is obligatorily naṣb. The sentence is affirmative with the excepted-from («الطُّلَّابُ») present, so «خَالِدًا» is manṣūb mustathnā. Reading it as badal (which is only allowed in negative complete exception) would be the wrong path here.

question_ar: فِي «مَا نَجَحَ إِلَّا مُجْتَهِدٌ»، مَا نَوْعُ الِاسْتِثْنَاءِ وَمَا إِعْرَابُ «مُجْتَهِدٌ»؟
question_en: In «مَا نَجَحَ إِلَّا مُجْتَهِدٌ», what type of exception is it, and how is «مُجْتَهِدٌ» parsed?
expected_answer: اسْتِثْنَاءٌ مُفَرَّغٌ؛ «مُجْتَهِدٌ» فَاعِلٌ مَرْفُوعٌ — exhaustive (mufarragh) exception; «مُجْتَهِدٌ» is fā‘il marfū‘.
expected_reasoning: When the speech is negative and the mustathnā minhu is omitted (mufarragh), إِلَّا is disregarded and the noun takes the case its grammatical position demands. Here the verb نَجَحَ needs a fā‘il, so «مُجْتَهِدٌ» is fā‘il marfū‘, not a manṣūb mustathnā. Forcing naṣb here would mis-case it because there is no excepted-from for it to be excluded from.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «غَيْرَ» وَمَا بَعْدَهَا فِي «جَاءَ القَوْمُ غَيْرَ وَاحِدٍ».
question_en: Parse «غَيْرَ» and what follows in «جَاءَ القَوْمُ غَيْرَ وَاحِدٍ».
expected_answer: «غَيْرَ» مُسْتَثْنًى مَنْصُوبٌ، وَ«وَاحِدٍ» مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ مَجْرُورٌ — «غَيْرَ» manṣūb mustathnā, «وَاحِدٍ» muḍāf ilayh.
expected_reasoning: With غَيْر/سِوَى, the EXCEPTION case falls on غَيْر itself (it takes the case the noun after إِلَّا would take), while the following noun is always its muḍāf ilayh in jarr. The construction is positive complete, so غَيْر is manṣūb (like a post-إِلَّا noun in naṣb); «وَاحِدٍ» is majrūr by iḍāfa. Putting naṣb on «وَاحِدٍ» would ignore the iḍāfa.

question_ar: حَوِّلْ «فَتَحَ القَائِدُ المَدِينَةَ» إِلَى المَبْنِيِّ لِلْمَجْهُولِ، وَأَعْرِبْ نَائِبَ الفَاعِلِ.
question_en: Convert «فَتَحَ القَائِدُ المَدِينَةَ» to passive and parse the deputy-agent.
expected_answer: «فُتِحَتِ المَدِينَةُ»؛ «المَدِينَةُ» نَائِبُ فَاعِلٍ مَرْفُوعٌ — «المَدِينَةُ» is nāʾib fāʿil marfū‘.
expected_reasoning: In the passive, the fā‘il is deleted, the verb's first letter takes ḍamma and the letter before the last takes kasra (فُتِحَ), and the former mafʿūl bih is promoted to nāʾib fāʿil in raf‘. «المَدِينَةُ», previously manṣūb object, becomes marfū‘ nāʾib fāʿil; the verb also takes the feminine tā’ to agree with it. Keeping it manṣūb would betray the promotion that defines the passive.

question_ar: كَيْفَ يُبْنَى الفِعْلُ الأَجْوَفُ «قَالَ» لِلْمَجْهُولِ، وَمَا التَّغْيِيرُ الصَّوْتِيُّ؟
question_en: How is the hollow verb «قَالَ» made passive, and what is the phonetic change?
expected_answer: يُقَالُ «قِيلَ»؛ تُقْلَبُ الأَلِفُ يَاءً وَتُكْسَرُ القَافُ — it becomes «قِيلَ»; the alif turns to yā’ and the qāf is given kasra.
expected_reasoning: Hollow verbs (middle-weak) in the past passive give kasra to the first sound letter and convert the medial weak letter to yā’: قَالَ → قِيلَ, بَاعَ → بِيعَ. The rule 'first letter ḍamma, pre-last kasra' is realized as iʿlāl here, so a naive ḍamma on the qāf (*قُولَ) is wrong. The deputy-agent then follows in raf‘.

question_ar: مَا عَلَامَةُ جَرِّ المَمْنُوعِ مِنَ الصَّرْفِ، وَمَثِّلْ بِـ«أَحْمَدَ»؟
question_en: What is the jarr sign of a diptote (mamnū‘ min al-ṣarf)? Illustrate with «أحمد».
expected_answer: يُجَرُّ بِالفَتْحَةِ نِيَابَةً عَنِ الكَسْرَةِ بِلَا تَنْوِينٍ، نَحْوَ «مَرَرْتُ بِأَحْمَدَ».
expected_reasoning: The diptote refuses tanwīn and is jarr by FATḤA in place of kasra (unless prefixed by ال or in iḍāfa). «أَحْمَدَ» is a proper noun on the verb pattern (af‘al), a cause of prevention, so «بِأَحْمَدَ» is majrūr by fatḥa, not kasra. Putting kasratayn on it (*بأحمدٍ) would treat a diptote as a triptote.

question_ar: لِمَ صُرِفَتْ «صَحَارِيَ» فِي بَعْضِ المَوَاضِعِ، وَمَتَى تُمْنَعُ؟ بَيِّنْ سِرَّ صِيغَةِ مُنْتَهَى الجُمُوعِ.
question_en: Why is «صحارٍ/صحاري» tricky, and when is the 'final-plural' pattern barred from ṣarf?
expected_answer: صِيغَةُ مُنْتَهَى الجُمُوعِ (مَفَاعِل/مَفَاعِيل) مَمْنُوعَةٌ مِنَ الصَّرْفِ لِلْعِلَّةِ الوَاحِدَةِ القَائِمَةِ مَقَامَ عِلَّتَيْنِ.
expected_reasoning: Plurals on the pattern of mafāʿil / mafāʿīl (ṣīghat muntahā al-jumūʿ) are diptotes because that pattern alone stands in for two causes. «مَسَاجِد، مَصَابِيح» are jarr by fatḥa without tanwīn. The manqūṣ ones like «صَحَارٍ» show the diptote effect by dropping the final yā’ and adding tanwīn al-‘iwaḍ in raf‘/jarr — a surface kasratayn that does NOT mean it is a triptote. This subtlety is exactly the kind of reasoning a surface scan gets wrong.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «ضَاحِكًا» فِي «أَقْبَلَ الطِّفْلُ ضَاحِكًا».
question_en: Parse «ضَاحِكًا» in «أَقْبَلَ الطِّفْلُ ضَاحِكًا».
expected_answer: حَالٌ مَنْصُوبٌ، وَصَاحِبُهُ «الطِّفْلُ» — ḥāl in naṣb; its ṣāḥib is «الطِّفْلُ».
expected_reasoning: The ḥāl is a naṣb descriptor stating the STATE of the doer (or object) at the time of the action; it is typically derived and indefinite, while its ṣāḥib is definite. «ضَاحِكًا» (manṣūb) describes how «الطِّفْلُ» (its definite ṣāḥib) was when he approached. It answers 'how/in what state', distinguishing it from a naʿt (which would follow the noun and agree in definiteness).

question_ar: فِي «جَاءَنِي زَيْدٌ وَالشَّمْسُ طَالِعَةٌ»، مَا نَوْعُ الحَالِ، وَمَا الرَّابِطُ؟
question_en: In «جَاءَنِي زَيْدٌ وَالشَّمْسُ طَالِعَةٌ», what kind of ḥāl is it, and what is the link?
expected_answer: حَالٌ جُمْلَةٌ اسْمِيَّةٌ، فِي مَحَلِّ نَصْبٍ، وَالرَّابِطُ الوَاوُ (وَاوُ الحَالِ).
expected_reasoning: A ḥāl may be a single word, a phrase, or a sentence. Here «الشَّمْسُ طَالِعَةٌ» is a nominal sentence functioning as ḥāl, fī maḥall naṣb, describing the circumstance of زَيْد's coming. The linking device is wāw al-ḥāl (a sentence-ḥāl links by wāw and/or a pronoun). Reading the wāw as ‘aṭf would wrongly coordinate two independent clauses.

question_ar: عَيِّنِ البَدَلَ وَنَوْعَهُ فِي «جَاءَ الأُسْتَاذُ خَالِدٌ» وَأَعْرِبْهُ.
question_en: Identify the badal and its type in «جَاءَ الأُسْتَاذُ خَالِدٌ» and parse it.
expected_answer: «خَالِدٌ» بَدَلٌ مُطَابِقٌ (بَدَلُ كُلٍّ مِنْ كُلٍّ) مَرْفُوعٌ، تَابِعٌ لِـ«الأُسْتَاذُ».
expected_reasoning: Badal is a tābi‘ that is the true intended word, the preceding noun being a paving for it; it matches its mubdal minhu in case. «خَالِدٌ» = «الأُسْتَاذُ» (the same person), so it is badal muṭābiq (kull min kull), marfū‘ following the marfū‘ «الأُسْتَاذُ» as fā‘il. It is not a naʿt (a name is not a description) nor a separate fā‘il.

question_ar: بَيِّنْ نَوْعَ البَدَلِ فِي «أَعْجَبَنِي الكِتَابُ أُسْلُوبُهُ»، وَأَعْرِبْهُ.
question_en: State the type of badal in «أَعْجَبَنِي الكِتَابُ أُسْلُوبُهُ» and parse it.
expected_answer: «أُسْلُوبُهُ» بَدَلُ اشْتِمَالٍ مَرْفُوعٌ، فِيهِ ضَمِيرٌ رَابِطٌ يَعُودُ عَلَى المُبْدَلِ مِنْهُ.
expected_reasoning: Badal al-ishtimāl is a part-of-the-meaning (not part-of-the-body) badal, and it requires a linking pronoun to the mubdal minhu. «أُسْلُوبُهُ» is an attribute the book 'comprises'; the هُ links it to «الكِتَابُ». It is marfū‘ following «الكِتَابُ» (fā‘il). Misreading it as badal ba‘ḍ (part-whole) would mistake a non-physical aspect for a portion.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «عَرَقًا» فِي «تَصَبَّبَ التِّلْمِيذُ عَرَقًا».
question_en: Parse «عَرَقًا» in «تَصَبَّبَ التِّلْمِيذُ عَرَقًا».
expected_answer: تَمْيِيزٌ مَنْصُوبٌ (مُحَوَّلٌ عَنِ الفَاعِلِ) — tamyīz in naṣb, transformed from the fā‘il.
expected_reasoning: Tamyīz removes ambiguity in a vague preceding word; here «عَرَقًا» clarifies WHAT poured. It is a tamyīz nisbah transformed from the fā‘il (the underlying sense is 'the pupil's sweat poured'). It is naṣb by fatḥa, indefinite. It is not a ḥāl (it specifies, not describes a state) and not a mafʿūl bih (تَصَبَّبَ is intransitive here).

question_ar: مَا الفَرْقُ بَيْنَ «عِنْدِي رِطْلٌ عَسَلًا» وَ«عِنْدِي رِطْلُ عَسَلٍ»؟
question_en: What is the difference between «عِنْدِي رِطْلٌ عَسَلًا» and «عِنْدِي رِطْلُ عَسَلٍ»?
expected_answer: الأُولَى «عَسَلًا» تَمْيِيزٌ مَنْصُوبٌ بَعْدَ مِقْدَارٍ، وَالثَّانِيَةُ «عَسَلٍ» مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ مَجْرُورٌ.
expected_reasoning: After measures/weights the specified noun may be naṣb as tamyīz (with tanwīn on the measure) or jarr as muḍāf ilayh (measure stripped of tanwīn). In «رِطْلٌ عَسَلًا», «رِطْلٌ» keeps tanwīn and «عَسَلًا» is tamyīz manṣūb; in «رِطْلُ عَسَلٍ», iḍāfa makes «عَسَلٍ» majrūr. The harakah/tanwīn pattern, not the lexeme, distinguishes the two structures.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «العِلْمِ» فِي «هَذَا كِتَابُ العِلْمِ».
question_en: Parse «العِلْمِ» in «هَذَا كِتَابُ العِلْمِ».
expected_answer: مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ مَجْرُورٌ — muḍāf ilayh in jarr.
expected_reasoning: In an iḍāfa, the second term is always majrūr and gives definiteness/specification to the first. «العِلْمِ» is muḍāf ilayh majrūr by kasra; «كِتَابُ» is the muḍāf, which therefore drops its own tanwīn and ال. The jarr here is by iḍāfa, not by a preposition.

question_ar: مَيِّزِ الإِضَافَةَ المَعْنَوِيَّةَ مِنَ اللَّفْظِيَّةِ فِي «حَسَنُ الخُلُقِ» وَ«بَابُ الدَّارِ».
question_en: Distinguish the moral (maʿnawiyyah) from the verbal (lafẓiyyah) iḍāfa in «حَسَنُ الخُلُقِ» and «بَابُ الدَّارِ».
expected_answer: «بَابُ الدَّارِ» إِضَافَةٌ مَعْنَوِيَّةٌ تُفِيدُ التَّعْرِيفَ، وَ«حَسَنُ الخُلُقِ» إِضَافَةٌ لَفْظِيَّةٌ لَا تُفِيدُ تَعْرِيفًا.
expected_reasoning: Moral iḍāfa (muḍāf is a non-descriptive noun) makes the muḍāf definite or specific: «بَابُ الدَّارِ» = the door of the house. Verbal/formal iḍāfa is a descriptive (ṣifah) annexed to its own object for lightness only — «حَسَنُ الخُلُقِ» stays indefinite-in-meaning and can take ال («الحَسَنُ الخُلُقِ»). Treating «حَسَنُ» as definite by its iḍāfa would mis-handle its naʿt agreement.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «المَسْجِدِ» فِي «ذَهَبْتُ إِلَى المَسْجِدِ».
question_en: Parse «المَسْجِدِ» in «ذَهَبْتُ إِلَى المَسْجِدِ».
expected_answer: اسْمٌ مَجْرُورٌ بِـ«إِلَى» وَعَلَامَةُ جَرِّهِ الكَسْرَةُ، وَالجَارُّ وَالمَجْرُورُ مُتَعَلِّقَانِ بِـ«ذَهَبْتُ».
expected_reasoning: A noun after a ḥarf jarr is majrūr; the preposition + its noun form a jār-majrūr attaching to the verb. «المَسْجِدِ» is majrūr by kasra after «إِلَى», and the phrase relates to «ذَهَبْتُ» as a place complement. Because it is governed by a preposition, no VERB gloss can be assigned to «المَسْجِدِ».

question_ar: عَلَامَ تَتَعَلَّقُ شِبْهُ الجُمْلَةِ «فِي الدَّارِ» فِي «الجَالِسُ فِي الدَّارِ أَخِي»؟
question_en: What does the phrase «فِي الدَّارِ» attach to in «الجَالِسُ فِي الدَّارِ أَخِي»?
expected_answer: تَتَعَلَّقُ بِاسْمِ الفَاعِلِ «الجَالِسُ» الَّذِي يَعْمَلُ عَمَلَ فِعْلِهِ.
expected_reasoning: A jār-majrūr must attach to a verb or a derivative that has verbal force. «الجَالِسُ» is an ism fā‘il (derivative) acting like its verb, so «فِي الدَّارِ» attaches to it as a place complement. Attaching it to «أَخِي» (a non-operating noun) would have no governor; the operating derivative is the correct anchor.

question_ar: فِي «قَامَ وَقَعَدَ زَيْدٌ»، مَا مَسْأَلَةُ التَّنَازُعِ، وَأَيُّ العَامِلَيْنِ أَوْلَى بِالعَمَلِ عِنْدَ البَصْرِيِّينَ؟
question_en: In «قَامَ وَقَعَدَ زَيْدٌ», what is the tanāzu‘ issue, and which governor do the Baṣrans prefer?
expected_answer: عَامِلَانِ تَنَازَعَا مَعْمُولًا وَاحِدًا «زَيْدٌ»؛ وَالبَصْرِيُّونَ يُعْمِلُونَ الثَّانِيَ «قَعَدَ»، فَيَكُونُ «زَيْدٌ» فَاعِلَهُ.
expected_reasoning: Tanāzu‘ is when two governors compete for one later element. The Baṣrans give precedence to the NEARER (second) governor for proximity, so «زَيْدٌ» is fā‘il of «قَعَدَ» (marfū‘), and «قَامَ» takes a hidden pronoun referring back. The Kūfans prefer the first. Either way a hidden subject must be supplied for the non-operating verb — a surface scan would leave one verb subjectless.

question_ar: تِلْمِيذٌ أَعْرَبَ «زَيْدًا» فِي «أَكْرَمْتُ وَأَكْرَمَنِي زَيْدٌ» مَنْصُوبًا لِلْأَوَّلِ. قَوِّمْ بِنَاءً عَلَى مَذْهَبِ البَصْرِيِّينَ.
question_en: A student parsed the shared element as manṣūb for the first governor in a tanāzu‘ case. Evaluate per the Baṣran view.
expected_answer: عَلَى مَذْهَبِ البَصْرِيِّينَ يُعْمَلُ الثَّانِي، فَيُرْفَعُ «زَيْدٌ» فَاعِلًا لِـ«أَكْرَمَنِي»، وَيُضْمَرُ مَفْعُولٌ لِلْأَوَّلِ.
expected_reasoning: With «أَكْرَمْتُ» (needs an object) and «أَكْرَمَنِي» (needs a subject) competing for «زَيْد», the Baṣrans operate the second: «زَيْدٌ» is fā‘il marfū‘ of «أَكْرَمَنِي», and the first verb «أَكْرَمْتُ» takes an implied object pronoun «أَكْرَمْتُهُ». The student's naṣb for the first violates the proximity rule and strands «أَكْرَمَنِي» without a subject.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «الوَاجِبَ» فِي «المُؤَدِّي الوَاجِبَ نَاجِحٌ».
question_en: Parse «الوَاجِبَ» in «المُؤَدِّي الوَاجِبَ نَاجِحٌ».
expected_answer: مَفْعُولٌ بِهِ لِاسْمِ الفَاعِلِ «المُؤَدِّي»، مَنْصُوبٌ — direct object of the ism fā‘il, manṣūb.
expected_reasoning: An ism fā‘il prefixed with ال works like its verb unconditionally and raises a fā‘il / governs a mafʿūl. «المُؤَدِّي» (the one who performs) governs «الوَاجِبَ» as its mafʿūl bih in naṣb. The whole is the mubtadaʾ; «نَاجِحٌ» is the khabar. Treating «الوَاجِبَ» as a muḍāf ilayh would wrongly make it majrūr.

question_ar: مَتَى يَعْمَلُ اسْمُ الفَاعِلِ المُجَرَّدُ مِنْ «أَلْ»؟ مَثِّلْ، وَأَعْرِبْ مَعْمُولَهُ.
question_en: When does an ism fā‘il without ال operate? Illustrate and parse its object.
expected_answer: يَعْمَلُ بِشَرْطَيْنِ: الِاعْتِمَادِ عَلَى اسْتِفْهَامٍ أَوْ نَفْيٍ أَوْ مَوْصُوفٍ أَوْ مُبْتَدَأٍ، وَدَلَالَتِهِ عَلَى الحَالِ أَوِ الِاسْتِقْبَالِ، نَحْوَ «أَقَائِمٌ الزَّيْدَانِ؟».
expected_reasoning: The bare ism fā‘il operates only if (1) it leans on an interrogative/negative/described-noun/mubtadaʾ and (2) it denotes the present or future. In «أَقَائِمٌ الزَّيْدَانِ؟», «قَائِمٌ» leans on the hamza of istifhām, so it raises «الزَّيْدَانِ» as a fā‘il (sadda masadd al-khabar), marfū‘ by alif (dual). Without these conditions it cannot govern, and any object reading would be ungrounded.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «الرَّجُلُ» فِي «نِعْمَ الرَّجُلُ أَبُو بَكْرٍ».
question_en: Parse «الرَّجُلُ» in «نِعْمَ الرَّجُلُ أَبُو بَكْرٍ».
expected_answer: فَاعِلُ «نِعْمَ» مَرْفُوعٌ، وَ«أَبُو بَكْرٍ» المَخْصُوصُ بِالمَدْحِ (مُبْتَدَأٌ خَبَرُهُ الجُمْلَةُ، أَوْ خَبَرٌ لِمُبْتَدَأٍ مَحْذُوفٍ).
expected_reasoning: نِعْمَ and بِئْسَ are frozen verbs of praise/blame whose fā‘il is either definite by ال or a tamyīz. «الرَّجُلُ» is the fā‘il marfū‘; «أَبُو بَكْرٍ» is al-makhṣūṣ bi-l-madḥ, parsed as a mubtadaʾ muʾakhkhar (with the نِعْمَ-sentence as its khabar). Reading «الرَّجُلُ» as a mubtadaʾ would miss that نِعْمَ is a verb demanding a fā‘il.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «خُلُقًا» فِي «نِعْمَ خُلُقًا الصِّدْقُ»، وَبَيِّنْ نَوْعَ الفَاعِلِ.
question_en: Parse «خُلُقًا» in «نِعْمَ خُلُقًا الصِّدْقُ» and state the type of fā‘il.
expected_answer: «خُلُقًا» تَمْيِيزٌ مَنْصُوبٌ مُفَسِّرٌ لِفَاعِلِ «نِعْمَ» المُضْمَرِ، وَ«الصِّدْقُ» المَخْصُوصُ بِالمَدْحِ.
expected_reasoning: When the fā‘il of نِعْمَ is a hidden pronoun, it is explained by a following naṣb tamyīz. «خُلُقًا» is that tamyīz manṣūb interpreting the concealed fā‘il (taqdīr: نِعْمَ هو خُلُقًا); «الصِّدْقُ» is al-makhṣūṣ. Calling «خُلُقًا» a ḥāl or mafʿūl would ignore the special tamyīz-explains-hidden-fā‘il structure of نِعْمَ/بِئْسَ.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «العَرَبَ» فِي «نَحْنُ — العَرَبَ — نُكْرِمُ الضَّيْفَ».
question_en: Parse «العَرَبَ» in «نَحْنُ — العَرَبَ — نُكْرِمُ الضَّيْفَ».
expected_answer: مَفْعُولٌ بِهِ مَنْصُوبٌ بِفِعْلٍ مَحْذُوفٍ تَقْدِيرُهُ «أَخُصُّ»، عَلَى الِاخْتِصَاصِ.
expected_reasoning: Ikhtiṣāṣ is a noun in naṣb after a (usually first-person) pronoun, governed by an obligatorily-deleted verb «أَخُصُّ/أَعْنِي», restricting the pronoun's reference. «العَرَبَ» is manṣūb mafʿūl bih of that hidden verb; «نَحْنُ» is the mubtadaʾ and «نُكْرِمُ…» its khabar. Reading «العَرَبَ» as badal of «نَحْنُ» is barred because badal cannot follow a speaker-pronoun this way; the deleted-verb naṣb is required.

question_ar: قَوِّمْ قَوْلَ مَنْ أَعْرَبَ «أَيُّهَا» فِي «أَنَا — أَيُّهَا العَبْدُ — فَقِيرٌ» مُنَادًى.
question_en: Evaluate parsing «أَيُّهَا» as a vocative in «أَنَا — أَيُّهَا العَبْدُ — فَقِيرٌ».
expected_answer: الإِعْرَابُ مَرْجُوحٌ هُنَا؛ الأَوْلَى أَنَّهُ مِنْ بَابِ الِاخْتِصَاصِ بِـ«أَيّ»، فَـ«أَيُّهَا» مَبْنِيٌّ عَلَى الضَّمِّ فِي مَحَلِّ نَصْبٍ عَلَى الِاخْتِصَاصِ.
expected_reasoning: «أَيّ» + ها + ال-noun after a pronoun, with no call/summons intent, is the ikhtiṣāṣ idiom, not nidāʾ; the meaning is restriction ('I — meaning the servant — am poor'), not calling. «أَيُّهَا» is mabnī ʿalā al-ḍamm fī maḥall naṣb ʿalā al-ikhtiṣāṣ, «العَبْدُ» a na‘t/badal marfū‘ on lafẓ. Forcing nidāʾ mistakes the discourse function despite the identical surface form.

question_ar: مَا عَلَامَةُ جَزْمِ الفِعْلِ المُضَارِعِ الصَّحِيحِ الآخِرِ، وَمَثِّلْ بِـ«يَكْتُبْ»؟
question_en: What is the jazm sign of a present verb with a sound final letter, e.g. «يكتب»?
expected_answer: السُّكُونُ، نَحْوَ «لَمْ يَكْتُبْ» — sukūn, e.g. «لَمْ يَكْتُبْ».
expected_reasoning: The original jazm sign is sukūn for the sound present verb. «لَمْ يَكْتُبْ» is majzūm by sukūn after «لَمْ». For weak-ended or the five verbs, deletion of the weak letter/nūn substitutes. So a surface sukūn after a jāzim signals jazm, not a pause.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «يَسْعَوْا» فِي «لَمْ يَسْعَوْا فِي الخَيْرِ».
question_en: Parse «يَسْعَوْا» in «لَمْ يَسْعَوْا فِي الخَيْرِ».
expected_answer: فِعْلٌ مُضَارِعٌ مَجْزُومٌ بِحَذْفِ النُّونِ، وَوَاوُ الجَمَاعَةِ فَاعِلٌ — majzūm by deleting the nūn; the wāw is the fā‘il.
expected_reasoning: «يَسْعَوْا» is one of the Five Verbs (al-af‘āl al-khamsa), which are jazm and naṣb by DELETING the nūn (their raf‘ sign). After «لَمْ» it is majzūm bi-ḥadhf al-nūn; the wāw al-jamā‘a is a subject pronoun. A naive expectation of sukūn would misidentify the jazm sign for this category.

question_ar: لِمَ بُنِيَ «اكْتُبْ» وَلَمْ يُعْرَبْ، وَعَلَامَ بُنِيَ؟
question_en: Why is «اكْتُبْ» built rather than declined, and on what?
expected_answer: لِأَنَّهُ فِعْلُ أَمْرٍ، وَالأَصْلُ فِي فِعْلِ الأَمْرِ البِنَاءُ، مَبْنِيٌّ عَلَى السُّكُونِ.
expected_reasoning: Only the present verb is muʿrab (when free of the emphasis/feminine nūn); the past and the imperative are inherently mabnī. «اكْتُبْ» is fi‘l amr, mabnī ʿalā al-sukūn (sound-ended). Asking for its 'iʿrāb case' is itself a category error — it has a building state, not a declension.

question_ar: مَا نَوْعُ الخَبَرِ فِي «المُؤْمِنُ يَصْدُقُ»، وَمَا مَحَلُّهُ؟
question_en: What is the type of khabar in «المُؤْمِنُ يَصْدُقُ», and its grammatical place?
expected_answer: خَبَرٌ جُمْلَةٌ فِعْلِيَّةٌ «يَصْدُقُ» فِي مَحَلِّ رَفْعٍ، وَالرَّابِطُ ضَمِيرٌ مُسْتَتِرٌ.
expected_reasoning: A khabar may be a single word, a phrase, or a sentence; a sentence-khabar is fī maḥall raf‘ and needs a linking pronoun to the mubtadaʾ. «يَصْدُقُ» contains a hidden subject (هو) referring to «المُؤْمِنُ», so the verbal sentence is the khabar in maḥall raf‘. Reading «يَصْدُقُ» as starting a fresh sentence would miss the rābiṭ that ties it to the mubtadaʾ.

question_ar: أَعْرِبِ الجُمْلَةَ «مَا زَالَ المَطَرُ نَازِلًا».
question_en: Parse the sentence «مَا زَالَ المَطَرُ نَازِلًا».
expected_answer: «مَا» نَافِيَةٌ، «زَالَ» فِعْلٌ نَاقِصٌ، «المَطَرُ» اسْمُهَا مَرْفُوعٌ، «نَازِلًا» خَبَرُهَا مَنْصُوبٌ.
expected_reasoning: «مَا زَالَ» is one of kāna's sisters (the istimrār group) and only works after a negative/quasi-negative «مَا». It raises its ism and naṣbs its khabar like كَانَ. «المَطَرُ» is ism marfū‘, «نَازِلًا» khabar manṣūb. The construction asserts continuity ('the rain kept falling'); reading «مَا» as a relative or interrogative would break the kāna-sister governance.

question_ar: اِبْنِ جُمْلَةً صَحِيحَةً تَحْتَوِي حَالًا مُفْرَدَةً وَصَاحِبَ حَالٍ نَكِرَةً مُسَوَّغًا، وَأَعْرِبِ الحَالَ.
question_en: Compose a correct sentence with a single-word ḥāl whose ṣāḥib is an indefinite noun with a valid licenser, and parse the ḥāl.
expected_answer: مِثَالٌ: «جَاءَنِي رَجُلٌ رَاكِبًا وَهُوَ يَضْحَكُ» — أَوْ بِتَقْدِيمِ الحَالِ «فِي الدَّارِ جَالِسًا رَجُلٌ»؛ «جَالِسًا» حَالٌ مَنْصُوبٌ.
expected_reasoning: The ḥāl's ṣāḥib is normally definite, but an indefinite ṣāḥib is licensed when (among other cases) the ḥāl precedes it or the noun is specified. In «فِي الدَّارِ جَالِسًا رَجُلٌ», fronting «جَالِسًا» (manṣūb ḥāl) before the indefinite «رَجُلٌ» is the licenser (musawwigh). A learner who demands a definite ṣāḥib unconditionally would wrongly reject this valid structure.

question_ar: تِلْمِيذٌ نَصَبَ «زَيْدٌ» فِي «مَا جَاءَ القَوْمُ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ» عَلَى الِاسْتِثْنَاءِ. قَوِّمْ.
question_en: A student put «زيد» in naṣb on exception in «مَا جَاءَ القَوْمُ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ». Evaluate.
expected_answer: يَجُوزُ فِيهِ وَجْهَانِ: النَّصْبُ عَلَى الِاسْتِثْنَاءِ وَالأَرْجَحُ البَدَلُ مِنَ «القَوْمُ» فَيُرْفَعُ «زَيْدٌ».
expected_reasoning: In a negative COMPLETE exception, the excepted noun may be naṣb (on exception) or follow the mustathnā minhu as badal in its case. Here «القَوْمُ» is marfū‘ fā‘il, so the preferred reading makes «زَيْدٌ» a badal marfū‘; naṣb is permitted but weaker. The student's naṣb is defensible but not the rājiḥ; flagging both shows the case is genuinely ambiguous — exactly a two-vote situation.

question_ar: كَمْ حَرْفًا مِنْ حُرُوفِ الجَرِّ فِي «مَرَرْتُ بِالبَيْتِ مِنَ الصَّبَاحِ»؟ وَأَعْرِبْ «الصَّبَاحِ».
question_en: How many prepositions are in «مَرَرْتُ بِالبَيْتِ مِنَ الصَّبَاحِ»? Parse «الصَّبَاحِ».
expected_answer: حَرْفَانِ: «البَاءُ» وَ«مِنْ»؛ وَ«الصَّبَاحِ» اسْمٌ مَجْرُورٌ بِـ«مِنْ» بِالكَسْرَةِ.
expected_reasoning: بِ and مِنْ are both ḥurūf jarr; each makes its noun majrūr. «الصَّبَاحِ» is majrūr by kasra after «مِنْ», forming a jār-majrūr with temporal sense. Counting «مِنَ» as anything but a preposition (e.g. confusing it with «مَنْ») would be the homograph error the harakah on the mīm prevents (kasra → preposition).

question_ar: بَيِّنْ أَثَرَ «لَنْ» وَ«لَمْ» عَلَى الفِعْلِ المُضَارِعِ مِنْ حَيْثُ الإِعْرَابُ وَالزَّمَنُ.
question_en: Explain the effect of «لَنْ» and «لَمْ» on the present verb in case and time.
expected_answer: «لَنْ» تَنْصِبُ المُضَارِعَ وَتَجْعَلُهُ مُسْتَقْبَلًا مَنْفِيًّا، وَ«لَمْ» تَجْزِمُهُ وَتَقْلِبُ زَمَنَهُ إِلَى المَاضِي المَنْفِيِّ.
expected_reasoning: «لَنْ» is a particle of naṣb (future negation): «لَنْ يَخِيبَ» = manṣūb, future. «لَمْ» is a particle of jazm that flips the present's TIME to the past: «لَمْ يَخِبْ» = majzūm, meaning 'he did not fail' (past). The crucial trap: «لَمْ + present form» must be glossed as PAST, never present — a surface 'present tense' reading is the documented failure mode.

question_ar: فِي «لَا تَيْأَسُوا» وَ«لَا يَيْأَسُونَ»، فَرِّقْ بَيْنَ «لَا» النَّاهِيَةِ وَ«لَا» النَّافِيَةِ بِالإِعْرَابِ.
question_en: In «لا تيأسوا» and «لا ييأسون», distinguish prohibitive لا from negating لا by iʿrāb.
expected_answer: «لَا تَيْأَسُوا» لَا نَاهِيَةٌ جَازِمَةٌ (الفِعْلُ مَجْزُومٌ بِحَذْفِ النُّونِ)، وَ«لَا يَيْأَسُونَ» لَا نَافِيَةٌ لَا عَمَلَ لَهَا (الفِعْلُ مَرْفُوعٌ بِثُبُوتِ النُّونِ).
expected_reasoning: Prohibitive لā governs (jazm) and gives a command-to-refrain; negating لā does not govern and merely denies. The DIAGNOSTIC is the nūn of the five verbs: deleted (مَجْزُوم) under prohibition «تَيْأَسُوا», retained (مَرْفُوع) under negation «يَيْأَسُونَ». The two are homographs in the bare لا; only the verb's ending and the meaning separate them — hence never auto-resolve.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ مَفْعُولَيْ «جَعَلَ» فِي «جَعَلَ الخَيَّاطُ الثَّوْبَ قَمِيصًا».
question_en: Parse the two objects of «جَعَلَ» in «جَعَلَ الخَيَّاطُ الثَّوْبَ قَمِيصًا».
expected_answer: «الثَّوْبَ» مَفْعُولٌ أَوَّلُ، «قَمِيصًا» مَفْعُولٌ ثَانٍ، كِلَاهُمَا مَنْصُوبٌ — verb of transformation taking two objects.
expected_reasoning: «جَعَلَ» of transformation (taṣyīr) is among ẓanna's sisters in taking two objects, but here the meaning is 'turned X into Y', not heart-conviction. Both «الثَّوْبَ» and «قَمِيصًا» are manṣūb objects, the second not originally a khabar of certainty but the result of transformation. Distinguishing taṣyīr-جعل from the rajḥān/yaqīn verbs matters for the SENSE even though both take two naṣb objects.

question_ar: اِبْنِ جُمْلَةً فِيهَا تَمْيِيزُ عَدَدٍ مُمَيِّزُهُ مَجْرُورٌ بِالإِضَافَةِ، وَأَعْرِبْهُ.
question_en: Compose a sentence with a number-tamyīz whose specifier is majrūr by iḍāfa, and parse it.
expected_answer: مِثَالٌ: «قَرَأْتُ خَمْسَةَ كُتُبٍ»؛ «كُتُبٍ» تَمْيِيزٌ مَجْرُورٌ بِالإِضَافَةِ.
expected_reasoning: For numbers 3–10, the tamyīz (ma‘dūd) is a plural majrūr by iḍāfa: «خَمْسَةَ كُتُبٍ». «كُتُبٍ» is tamyīz majrūr (not manṣūb) here — contrasting with 11–99, where the tamyīz is a singular manṣūb («خَمْسَةَ عَشَرَ كِتَابًا»). A blanket 'tamyīz is always manṣūb' rule is wrong; the number's range decides the case.

question_ar: مَا نَوْعُ المَفْعُولِ المُطْلَقِ فِي «دُرْتُ دَوْرَتَيْنِ»؟
question_en: What type of maf‘ūl muṭlaq is in «دُرْتُ دَوْرَتَيْنِ»?
expected_answer: مَفْعُولٌ مُطْلَقٌ مُبَيِّنٌ لِلْعَدَدِ، مَنْصُوبٌ بِاليَاءِ لِأَنَّهُ مُثَنًّى.
expected_reasoning: A maf‘ūl muṭlaq can confirm, specify type, or COUNT the action's number. «دَوْرَتَيْنِ» counts the turning, hence mubayyin lil-‘adad. It is manṣūb by yā’ because it is a dual (mathnā). Reading it as a mere taʾkīd would miss the counting function the dual signals.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «قَلَمِي» فِي «هَذَا قَلَمِي».
question_en: Parse «قَلَمِي» in «هَذَا قَلَمِي».
expected_answer: «قَلَم» خَبَرٌ مَرْفُوعٌ مُضَافٌ مَنَعَ مِنْ ظُهُورِ الضَّمَّةِ اشْتِغَالُ المَحَلِّ، وَ«يَاءُ المُتَكَلِّمِ» مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ مَبْنِيٌّ فِي مَحَلِّ جَرٍّ.
expected_reasoning: The speaker's yā’ (yā’ al-mutakallim) is a muḍāf ilayh, mabnī fī maḥall jarr, and the noun before it is a muḍāf whose case-ending is estimated (muqaddar) because the position is occupied by the kasra demanded for the yā’. «قَلَمِي» is khabar marfū‘ taqdīran. Reading the kasra as a real jarr sign on «قَلَم» would mis-case the khabar.

question_ar: قَوِّمْ إِعْرَابَ مَنْ جَعَلَ «أَخُوكَ» فِي «مَرَرْتُ بِزَيْدٍ أَخِيكَ» مَرْفُوعًا.
question_en: Evaluate parsing «أخوك» as marfū‘ in «مَرَرْتُ بِزَيْدٍ أَخِيكَ».
expected_answer: الإِعْرَابُ خَطَأٌ؛ الصَّوَابُ «أَخِيكَ» بَدَلٌ مَجْرُورٌ تَابِعٌ لِـ«زَيْدٍ».
expected_reasoning: Badal follows its mubdal minhu in CASE. «زَيْدٍ» is majrūr by «بِ», so the badal «أَخِيكَ» must also be majrūr — shown by yā’ (one of the Five Nouns). Raising it to «أَخُوكَ» breaks the tābi‘ agreement; the badal cannot take a case independent of the noun it replaces.

question_ar: أَعْرِبِ اسْمَ «لَا» فِي «لَا صَاحِبَ خَيْرٍ مَمْقُوتٌ»، وَبَيِّنْ لِمَ أُعْرِبَ وَلَمْ يُبْنَ.
question_en: Parse the noun of لا in «لَا صَاحِبَ خَيْرٍ مَمْقُوتٌ» and say why it is declined, not built.
expected_answer: «صَاحِبَ» اسْمُ «لَا» مَنْصُوبٌ (لِأَنَّهُ مُضَافٌ)، وَ«خَيْرٍ» مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ مَجْرُورٌ، وَ«مَمْقُوتٌ» خَبَرُهَا.
expected_reasoning: The noun of لā li-nafy al-jins is BUILT on fatḥ only when it is a single (mufrad) noun. If it is a muḍāf (or resembles one), it is declined-manṣūb. «صَاحِبَ» is a muḍāf to «خَيْرٍ», so it is manṣūb (not mabnī) ism lā; «خَيْرٍ» majrūr by iḍāfa; «مَمْقُوتٌ» khabar marfū‘. Building «صَاحِبَ» on fatḥ would ignore the iḍāfa exception.

question_ar: لِمَ مُنِعَتْ «مَكَّةُ» مِنَ الصَّرْفِ؟
question_en: Why is «مكة» barred from ṣarf?
expected_answer: لِلْعَلَمِيَّةِ وَالتَّأْنِيثِ (تَأْنِيثٍ مَعْنَوِيٍّ لِبَلْدَةٍ) — for being a proper noun + feminine.
expected_reasoning: A proper noun that is also feminine (by meaning, as a place/town name) has two causes of prevention, so it is a diptote: «مَكَّةُ» is raf‘ by ḍamma without tanwīn, jarr by fatḥa. «صَلَّيْتُ فِي مَكَّةَ» shows jarr by fatḥa. Adding tanwīn (*مكةٍ) would treat a two-cause proper name as a triptote.

question_ar: عَيِّنِ المَعْمُولَ المُتَنَازَعَ فِيهِ وَأَعْرِبْهُ فِي «أَعْطَيْتُ وَكَسَوْتُ الفَقِيرَ».
question_en: Identify and parse the contested element in «أَعْطَيْتُ وَكَسَوْتُ الفَقِيرَ».
expected_answer: المُتَنَازَعُ فِيهِ «الفَقِيرَ»؛ يُعْمَلُ الثَّانِي «كَسَوْتُ» فَهُوَ مَفْعُولُهُ مَنْصُوبٌ، وَيُضْمَرُ لِلْأَوَّلِ.
expected_reasoning: Both «أَعْطَيْتُ» and «كَسَوْتُ» seek «الفَقِيرَ» as an object — a tanāzu‘ over a mafʿūl. On the Baṣran preference the nearer (second) verb operates: «الفَقِيرَ» is mafʿūl bih manṣūb of «كَسَوْتُ», and the first verb «أَعْطَيْتُ» takes an implied object pronoun «أَعْطَيْتُهُ». A surface scan giving the object to «أَعْطَيْتُ» alone would strand «كَسَوْتُ».

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «حَقُّهُ» فِي «المَظْلُومُ حَقُّهُ مَرْدُودٌ».
question_en: Parse «حَقُّهُ» in «المَظْلُومُ حَقُّهُ مَرْدُودٌ».
expected_answer: «حَقُّهُ» نَائِبُ فَاعِلٍ لِاسْمِ المَفْعُولِ «المَظْلُومِ»... بَلِ الأَصَحُّ: «المَظْلُومُ» مُبْتَدَأٌ، وَاسْمُ المَفْعُولِ يَعْمَلُ عَمَلَ فِعْلِهِ المَبْنِيِّ لِلْمَجْهُولِ فَيَرْفَعُ نَائِبَ فَاعِلٍ «حَقُّهُ» مَرْفُوعٌ.
expected_reasoning: An ism mafʿūl works like its passive verb and raises a nāʾib fā‘il. In «المَظْلُومُ حَقُّهُ مَرْدُودٌ», take «المَظْلُومُ» as mubtadaʾ, «مَرْدُودٌ» (ism mafʿūl) as khabar that raises «حَقُّهُ» as its nāʾib fā‘il marfū‘ — i.e. 'the wronged one, whose right is returned'. The operating derivative governs raf‘ on what would be the verb's deputy-agent; reading «حَقُّهُ» as a second mubtadaʾ would miss the ism mafʿūl's verbal force.

question_ar: قَوِّمْ قَوْلَ مَنْ بَنَى الفِعْلَ اللَّازِمَ «جَلَسَ» لِلْمَجْهُولِ فِي «جُلِسَ فِي الدَّارِ».
question_en: Evaluate making the intransitive verb «جلس» passive in «جُلِسَ فِي الدَّارِ».
expected_answer: جَائِزٌ؛ يُنَابُ عَنِ الفَاعِلِ بِالجَارِّ وَالمَجْرُورِ «فِي الدَّارِ»، أَوْ بِالمَصْدَرِ أَوِ الظَّرْفِ.
expected_reasoning: An intransitive verb has no mafʿūl bih to promote, but it CAN be made passive, and the nāʾib fā‘il is then a jār-majrūr, a ẓarf, or a maṣdar. In «جُلِسَ فِي الدَّارِ», «فِي الدَّارِ» stands in for the agent (the place is the deputy). The claim that intransitives cannot be passivized is wrong; only the type of deputy-agent changes.

question_ar: مَا الإِعْرَابُ الأَصْلِيُّ، وَمَا الإِعْرَابُ الفَرْعِيُّ؟ مَثِّلْ لِكُلٍّ بِمِثَالٍ.
question_en: What is original vs. sub-sign iʿrāb? Give one example of each.
expected_answer: الأَصْلِيُّ بِالحَرَكَاتِ (ضَمَّةٌ/فَتْحَةٌ/كَسْرَةٌ/سُكُونٌ) كَـ«الطَّالِبُ»، وَالفَرْعِيُّ بِالحُرُوفِ أَوِ الحَذْفِ كَـ«المُعَلِّمُونَ» وَ«لَمْ يَدْعُ».
expected_reasoning: The default (aṣlī) iʿrāb uses ḥarakāt: ḍamma raf‘, fatḥa naṣb, kasra jarr, sukūn jazm — e.g. «الطَّالِبُ» raf‘ by ḍamma. Sub-signs (far‘ī) appear in seven categories (duals, sound plurals, Five Nouns, Five Verbs, diptotes, weak-ended nouns/verbs) using letters or deletion — «المُعَلِّمُونَ» raf‘ by wāw; «لَمْ يَدْعُ» jazm by deleting the wāw. Confusing the two leads to wrong sign assignment.

question_ar: مَا الفَرْقُ بَيْنَ الحَالِ وَالتَّمْيِيزِ فِي «بِعْتُ القُطْنَ مَنْدُوفًا» وَ«اشْتَرَيْتُ رِطْلًا قُطْنًا»؟
question_en: What distinguishes ḥāl from tamyīz in «بِعْتُ القُطْنَ مَنْدُوفًا» vs. «اشْتَرَيْتُ رِطْلًا قُطْنًا»?
expected_answer: «مَنْدُوفًا» حَالٌ (وَصْفٌ لِهَيْئَةِ القُطْنِ)، وَ«قُطْنًا» تَمْيِيزٌ (يُزِيلُ إِبْهَامَ المِقْدَارِ).
expected_reasoning: The ḥāl describes a STATE and is derived/adjectival; the tamyīz removes the ambiguity of a vague quantity/word and is usually a jāmid noun. «مَنْدُوفًا» (carded) states the cotton's condition → ḥāl manṣūb. «قُطْنًا» after the measure «رِطْلًا» specifies the vague amount → tamyīz manṣūb. Swapping their analyses misreads description for specification.

question_ar: مَتَى يَجِبُ تَقْدِيمُ الخَبَرِ عَلَى المُبْتَدَأِ؟ اذْكُرْ مَوْضِعًا وَمَثِّلْ.
question_en: When must the khabar precede the mubtadaʾ? State one case and illustrate.
expected_answer: يَجِبُ تَقْدِيمُهُ إِذَا اتَّصَلَ بِالمُبْتَدَأِ ضَمِيرٌ يَعُودُ عَلَى الخَبَرِ، نَحْوَ «فِي البَيْتِ صَاحِبُهُ».
expected_reasoning: Among the obligatory-fronting cases is when the mubtadaʾ carries a pronoun referring back to the khabar; fronting prevents the pronoun preceding its referent. In «فِي البَيْتِ صَاحِبُهُ», the هُ in «صَاحِبُهُ» (mubtadaʾ muʾakhkhar) refers to «البَيْتِ» inside the fronted khabar «فِي البَيْتِ». Leaving it in default order would put the pronoun before its antecedent — a banned ‘awd structure.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «خَوْفَ» فِي «تَرَكْتُ المَعْصِيَةَ خَوْفَ اللهِ».
question_en: Parse «خَوْفَ» in «تَرَكْتُ المَعْصِيَةَ خَوْفَ اللهِ».
expected_answer: مَفْعُولٌ لِأَجْلِهِ مَنْصُوبٌ وَهُوَ مُضَافٌ، وَ«اللهِ» مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ مَجْرُورٌ.
expected_reasoning: «خَوْفَ» is a maṣdar qalbī giving the MOTIVE for leaving sin, matching the doer and time of تَرَكْتُ → maf‘ūl li-ajlih manṣūb. It is added to «اللهِ» (muḍāf ilayh majrūr). Even when the maf‘ūl li-ajlih is a muḍāf it stays manṣūb; it is not a maf‘ūl muṭlaq (different root from the verb) nor a ḥāl.

question_ar: هَلْ يُعْرَبُ الفِعْلُ المَاضِي؟ وَمَا حُكْمُ «كَتَبَ»؟
question_en: Is the past verb declined? What is the status of «كتب»?
expected_answer: لَا يُعْرَبُ؛ المَاضِي مَبْنِيٌّ دَائِمًا، وَ«كَتَبَ» مَبْنِيٌّ عَلَى الفَتْحِ.
expected_reasoning: The past verb is always mabnī, never muʿrab: on fatḥ by default («كَتَبَ»), on sukūn with a raf‘ pronoun («كَتَبْتُ»), on ḍamm with wāw al-jamā‘a («كَتَبُوا»). It has no case. Asking 'is كَتَبَ marfū‘ or manṣūb?' is a category error — only the present verb is declinable.

question_ar: بَيِّنِ الفَرْقَ بَيْنَ «بَاءِ» السَّبَبِيَّةِ وَ«بَاءِ» الِاسْتِعَانَةِ، وَمَا أَثَرُهُ عَلَى المَعْنَى؟
question_en: Distinguish the bā’ of cause from the bā’ of instrument, and its effect on meaning.
expected_answer: بَاءُ السَّبَبِيَّةِ تُفِيدُ العِلَّةَ («أُخِذَ بِذَنْبِهِ») وَبَاءُ الِاسْتِعَانَةِ تُفِيدُ الآلَةَ («كَتَبْتُ بِالقَلَمِ»)، وَكِلَاهُمَا حَرْفُ جَرٍّ.
expected_reasoning: The bā’ is a single preposition with several meanings; both make the noun majrūr but differ in sense. Sababiyyah gives the CAUSE: «بِذَنْبِهِ» = 'because of his sin'. Istiʿānah gives the TOOL: «بِالقَلَمِ» = 'with the pen'. The case (jarr) is identical; only context picks the meaning, so the gloss of the bā’ itself is context-decided, not fixed.

question_ar: لِمَ حُذِفَ التَّنْوِينُ مِنْ «كِتَابُ» فِي «كِتَابُ اللهِ»؟
question_en: Why is the tanwīn dropped from «كتاب» in «كتاب الله»?
expected_answer: لِأَنَّهُ مُضَافٌ، وَالمُضَافُ يُحْذَفُ تَنْوِينُهُ (وَنُونُ تَثْنِيَتِهِ وَجَمْعِهِ) عِنْدَ الإِضَافَةِ.
expected_reasoning: A muḍāf is, in effect, in construct with its muḍāf ilayh and so loses its tanwīn and any dual/plural nūn, and may not take ال (definiteness comes from the second term). «كِتَابُ» as muḍāf to «اللهِ» drops tanwīn; «اللهِ» is muḍāf ilayh majrūr. Keeping the tanwīn («كِتَابٌ اللهِ») would break the iḍāfa bond.

question_ar: اِبْنِ جُمْلَةً يَكُونُ فِيهَا الِاسْتِثْنَاءُ مُنْقَطِعًا، وَأَعْرِبِ المُسْتَثْنَى.
question_en: Compose a sentence with a disconnected (munqaṭi‘) exception and parse the excepted noun.
expected_answer: مِثَالٌ: «حَضَرَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَّا كُتُبَهُمْ»؛ «كُتُبَهُمْ» مُسْتَثْنًى مُنْقَطِعٌ مَنْصُوبٌ.
expected_reasoning: A disconnected exception is when the excepted noun is NOT of the same genus as the excepted-from. Books are not a kind of students, so «كُتُبَهُمْ» is mustathnā munqaṭi‘, obligatorily manṣūb in the Ḥijāzī usage ('the students came, but their books [did not]'). Mistaking it for a connected exception (and allowing badal) would ignore the genus mismatch that forces naṣb.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «مَا» فِي «بِئْسَ مَا فَعَلْتَ».
question_en: Parse «مَا» in «بِئْسَ مَا فَعَلْتَ».
expected_answer: «مَا» نَكِرَةٌ تَامَّةٌ فِي مَحَلِّ نَصْبٍ تَمْيِيزٌ، فَاعِلُ «بِئْسَ» مُضْمَرٌ يُفَسِّرُهُ التَّمْيِيزُ.
expected_reasoning: After نِعْمَ/بِئْسَ, «مَا» is commonly a complete indefinite (nakirah tāmmah) functioning as a naṣb tamyīz that interprets the hidden fā‘il, while al-makhṣūṣ is the following clause. In «بِئْسَ مَا فَعَلْتَ», «مَا» is the tamyīz fī maḥall naṣb; the concealed fā‘il of بِئْسَ is what it explains. Reading «مَا» as a relative object would miss its tamyīz role specific to praise/blame verbs.

question_ar: قَوِّمْ إِعْرَابَ مَنْ جَعَلَ «وَحْدَهُ» فِي «جَاءَ زَيْدٌ وَحْدَهُ» مَفْعُولًا مُطْلَقًا.
question_en: Evaluate parsing «وَحْدَهُ» as a maf‘ūl muṭlaq in «جَاءَ زَيْدٌ وَحْدَهُ».
expected_answer: الأَرْجَحُ أَنَّهُ حَالٌ مَنْصُوبٌ (مَصْدَرٌ نَائِبٌ عَنِ اسْمِ الفَاعِلِ بِمَعْنَى مُنْفَرِدًا)، لَا مَفْعُولٌ مُطْلَقٌ.
expected_reasoning: «وَحْدَهُ» is a maṣdar used in the place of a derived ḥāl, meaning 'alone/solely', describing زَيْد's state when he came → ḥāl manṣūb (the majority view). It is not a maf‘ūl muṭlaq because it neither shares the root of «جَاءَ» nor confirms/counts the verb. The maf‘ūl-muṭlaq reading fails the same-root test that defines that category.

question_ar: مَا عَلَامَةُ رَفْعِ المُثَنَّى، وَمَثِّلْ بِـ«مُسْلِمَانِ»؟
question_en: What is the raf‘ sign of the dual? Illustrate with «مسلمان».
expected_answer: الأَلِفُ، نَحْوَ «جَاءَ المُسْلِمَانِ» — alif, e.g. «جَاءَ المُسْلِمَانِ».
expected_reasoning: The dual is raf‘ by alif and naṣb/jarr by yā’. «المُسْلِمَانِ» as fā‘il is marfū‘ by alif (with the nūn as a substitute for tanwīn). A surface ḍamma expectation is wrong for duals — the alif IS the raf‘ sign (a far‘ī/sub-sign).

question_ar: بَيِّنْ كَيْفَ تَجْزِمُ «إِنْ» الشَّرْطِيَّةُ فِعْلَيْنِ، وَأَعْرِبْهُمَا فِي «إِنْ تَجْتَهِدْ تَنْجَحْ».
question_en: Explain how conditional «إِنْ» causes jazm of two verbs, and parse them in «إِنْ تَجْتَهِدْ تَنْجَحْ».
expected_answer: «تَجْتَهِدْ» فِعْلُ الشَّرْطِ مَجْزُومٌ، وَ«تَنْجَحْ» جَوَابُ الشَّرْطِ مَجْزُومٌ، كِلَاهُمَا بِالسُّكُونِ.
expected_reasoning: A conditional particle (إِنْ) is a jāzim that governs TWO present verbs: the condition (fi‘l al-sharṭ) and its answer (jawāb al-sharṭ). Both «تَجْتَهِدْ» and «تَنْجَحْ» are majzūm by sukūn. The conditional/relative distinction matters: only because the second clause is an apodosis is «إِنْ» read as a jāzim conditional rather than something else — the apodosis decides the reading.

question_ar: فِي «غَيْرُ المَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ»، كَيْفَ اكْتَسَبَتْ «غَيْرُ» نَوْعًا مِنَ التَّعْرِيفِ مَعَ كَوْنِهَا شَدِيدَةَ الإِبْهَامِ؟
question_en: In «غَيْرُ المَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ», how did «غير» gain a kind of definiteness despite being deeply vague?
expected_answer: اكْتَسَبَتْ «غَيْرُ» تَعْرِيفًا (أَوْ تَخْصِيصًا) بِإِضَافَتِهَا إِلَى المَعْرِفَةِ «المَغْضُوبِ»، فَصَحَّ وَصْفُ المَعْرِفَةِ بِهَا.
expected_reasoning: «غَيْر» and «مِثْل» are extremely indefinite words that nonetheless take partial definiteness from being annexed to a definite muḍāf ilayh — that is how «غَيْرُ» (added to the definite «المَغْضُوبِ») can describe the definite «الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ». «المَغْضُوبِ» is muḍāf ilayh majrūr. Treating «غَيْر» as fully indefinite would block its naʿt agreement with a definite antecedent — a subtle iḍāfa-definiteness point.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «لَيْسَ» وَمَعْمُولَيْهَا فِي «لَيْسَ الغِنَى كَثْرَةَ المَالِ».
question_en: Parse «لَيْسَ» and its two governed words in «لَيْسَ الغِنَى كَثْرَةَ المَالِ».
expected_answer: «لَيْسَ» فِعْلٌ نَاقِصٌ، «الغِنَى» اسْمُهَا مَرْفُوعٌ بِضَمَّةٍ مُقَدَّرَةٍ، «كَثْرَةَ» خَبَرُهَا مَنْصُوبٌ وَهُوَ مُضَافٌ.
expected_reasoning: «لَيْسَ» is a sister of kāna negating the predicate: it raises the ism and naṣbs the khabar. «الغِنَى» is ism laysa, marfū‘ by an estimated ḍamma (maqṣūr noun). «كَثْرَةَ» is khabar laysa manṣūb and a muḍāf to «المَالِ» (majrūr). Reading «الغِنَى» as definite-by-ال and leaving «كَثْرَةَ» marfū‘ would ignore laysa's naṣb on the khabar.

question_ar: قَوِّمْ إِعْرَابَ مَنْ جَرَّ «شِبْرًا» فِي «اِزْدَادَ النَّهْرُ شِبْرًا».
question_en: Evaluate parsing «شبرا» as majrūr in «اِزْدَادَ النَّهْرُ شِبْرًا».
expected_answer: الجَرُّ خَطَأٌ؛ الصَّوَابُ «شِبْرًا» تَمْيِيزٌ مَنْصُوبٌ مُحَوَّلٌ عَنِ الفَاعِلِ.
expected_reasoning: After verbs of increase/decrease the specified amount is a tamyīz manṣūb, often transformed from the fā‘il (taqdīr: 'the river's [span] increased by a hand-span'). «شِبْرًا» is tamyīz manṣūb, not majrūr — there is no preposition or iḍāfa to justify jarr. The jarr reading invents a governor that is not present.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ بَدَلَ البَعْضِ فِي «أَكَلْتُ الرَّغِيفَ نِصْفَهُ».
question_en: Parse the part-whole badal in «أَكَلْتُ الرَّغِيفَ نِصْفَهُ».
expected_answer: «نِصْفَهُ» بَدَلُ بَعْضٍ مِنْ كُلٍّ مَنْصُوبٌ، فِيهِ ضَمِيرٌ رَابِطٌ، تَابِعٌ لِـ«الرَّغِيفَ».
expected_reasoning: Badal ba‘ḍ min kull names a genuine PORTION of the mubdal minhu and must carry a linking pronoun. «نِصْفَهُ» (half of it) is a physical part of «الرَّغِيفَ», so badal ba‘ḍ manṣūb (following the manṣūb mafʿūl «الرَّغِيفَ»), with هُ as the rābiṭ. Confusing it with badal ishtimāl (a non-physical aspect) would misclassify a true part as a comprised attribute.

question_ar: اِبْنِ جُمْلَةً يَنُوبُ فِيهَا غَيْرُ المَصْدَرِ عَنِ المَفْعُولِ المُطْلَقِ، وَأَعْرِبْ النَّائِبَ.
question_en: Compose a sentence where something other than the maṣdar stands in for the maf‘ūl muṭlaq, and parse it.
expected_answer: مِثَالٌ: «اجْتَهَدْتُ كُلَّ الِاجْتِهَادِ»؛ «كُلَّ» نَائِبٌ عَنِ المَفْعُولِ المُطْلَقِ مَنْصُوبٌ، وَهُوَ مُضَافٌ.
expected_reasoning: Several things may substitute for the maṣdar as maf‘ūl muṭlaq: «كُلّ/بَعْض» added to it, its adjective, its number, a synonym, or a demonstrative. «كُلَّ الِاجْتِهَادِ» — «كُلَّ» is nāʾib ‘an al-maf‘ūl al-muṭlaq manṣūb and muḍāf to «الِاجْتِهَادِ» (majrūr). Insisting the maf‘ūl muṭlaq must be the bare maṣdar would wrongly reject these recognized substitutes.

question_ar: مَتَى يُصْرَفُ المَمْنُوعُ مِنَ الصَّرْفِ فَيُجَرُّ بِالكَسْرَةِ؟ مَثِّلْ بِمَوْضِعَيْنِ.
question_en: When is a diptote re-enabled for ṣarf so it takes a kasra in jarr? Give two cases.
expected_answer: يُصْرَفُ إِذَا اقْتَرَنَ بِـ«أَلْ» أَوْ أُضِيفَ، نَحْوَ «صَلَّيْتُ فِي المَسَاجِدِ» وَ«فِي مَسَاجِدِ المَدِينَةِ».
expected_reasoning: A diptote regains the kasra of jarr (and tanwīn is irrelevant) when prefixed by ال or placed in iḍāfa. «المَسَاجِدِ» (with ال) and «مَسَاجِدِ المَدِينَةِ» (muḍāf) are both jarr by KASRA, despite the muntahā al-jumūʿ pattern. So a rule 'this pattern is always jarr by fatḥa' is wrong — ال and iḍāfa flip it back to kasra. This is exactly where a surface-pattern heuristic fails.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «زَيْدًا» وَ«قَائِمًا» فِي «ظَنَنْتُ زَيْدًا قَائِمًا».
question_en: Parse زَيْدًا and قَائِمًا in «ظَنَنْتُ زَيْدًا قَائِمًا».
expected_answer: Both are manṣūb: زيدًا = mafʿūl awwal, قائمًا = mafʿūl thānin (the original mubtadaʾ and khabar that ẓanna rendered manṣūb).
expected_reasoning: ẓanna is of أفعال القلوب, which enter a nominal sentence and make BOTH mubtadaʾ and khabar two manṣūb objects; so قائمًا is the second object, completing 'I thought Zayd [to be] standing'.
wrong_reasoning_trap: Both are manṣūb because زيدًا is mafʿūl bih and قائمًا is a ḥāl describing Zayd's state.
why_trap_wrong: Right ending, wrong path: قائمًا is the SECOND object of ẓanna, not a ḥāl. The ḥāl reading makes زيد the sole object and changes the meaning to 'I thought OF Zayd while [I was] standing' — a different sentence; it also fails when the second object is jāmid (ظننته زيدًا).

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «رَجُلَ» فِي «لَا رَجُلَ فِي الدَّارِ».
question_en: Parse رَجُلَ in «لَا رَجُلَ فِي الدَّارِ».
expected_answer: مبني على الفتح في محل نصب، اسم لا النافية للجنس — built (mabnī) on fatḥ, occupying the naṣb slot as the ism of lā.
expected_reasoning: lā nāfiyah lil-jins works like inna; a mufrad ism (not muḍāf, not shibh muḍāf) is MABNĪ on the sign it would take in naṣb (fatḥ here), not muʿrab.
wrong_reasoning_trap: رجلَ is manṣūb (muʿrab) as the ism of lā, and the fatḥa is its naṣb sign.
why_trap_wrong: Right that it ends in fatḥa, wrong that it is muʿrab/manṣūb: a mufrad ism lā is BUILT on fatḥ, not declined. The trap collapses on a muḍāf ism (لا صاحبَ جودٍ ممقوتٌ) which IS muʿrab manṣūb — so the wrong rule mispredicts there.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «زَيْدٌ» فِي «مَا قَامَ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ».
question_en: Parse زَيْدٌ in «مَا قَامَ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ».
expected_answer: فاعل مرفوع — fāʿil marfūʿ (istithnāʾ mufarragh: إلا has no effect; زيد takes the role its position requires).
expected_reasoning: This is istithnāʾ mufarragh (negative, no mustathnā minhu): إلا is cancelled grammatically, so the noun after it takes the iʿrāb the bare sentence demands — here the fāʿil of قام → marfūʿ.
wrong_reasoning_trap: زيدٌ is marfūʿ as a mustathnā on badaliyyah, because the kalām is tāmm manfī and the mustathnā in tāmm manfī may follow the mustathnā minhu in raf'.
why_trap_wrong: Right ending, wrong path: there is NO mustathnā minhu, so the kalām is mufarragh (nāqiṣ), not tāmm. Badal needs a stated mustathnā minhu; the marfūʿ here is fāʿiliyya, not badaliyya.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «نَفْسًا» فِي «طَابَ زَيْدٌ نَفْسًا».
question_en: Parse نَفْسًا in «طَابَ زَيْدٌ نَفْسًا».
expected_answer: تمييز منصوب — tamyīz manṣūb (tamyīz al-nisba, convertible to the fāʿil).
expected_reasoning: نفسًا is jāmid and removes the ambiguity of the nisba طاب; it is convertible to a fāʿil (طابت نفسُ زيدٍ), the defining test of tamyīz al-nisba → manṣūb.
wrong_reasoning_trap: نفسًا is ḥāl manṣūb, describing the state Zayd was in when he became good.
why_trap_wrong: Right ending, wrong path: ḥāl is normally mushtaqq and describes a doer's state; نفسًا is jāmid and answers 'in what respect?' Its convertibility to the fāʿil (طابت نفسُه) proves tamyīz, not ḥāl.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «أَحْمَدَ» فِي «مَرَرْتُ بِأَحْمَدَ».
question_en: Parse أَحْمَدَ in «مَرَرْتُ بِأَحْمَدَ».
expected_answer: اسم مجرور بالباء، وعلامة جره الفتحة نيابة عن الكسرة لأنه ممنوع من الصرف — majrūr with FATḤA substituting for kasra (diptote), no tanwīn.
expected_reasoning: أحمد is mamnūʿ min al-ṣarf (ʿalamiyya + wazn al-fiʿl); in jarr a diptote takes fatḥa as the jarr sign and carries no tanwīn.
wrong_reasoning_trap: أحمدَ ends in fatḥa, so the bi here has put it in the naṣb-shaped form that proper names take after prepositions.
why_trap_wrong: Right ending, wrong path: bi is ALWAYS a ḥarf jarr and never governs naṣb. The fatḥa is the JARR sign by niyāba in a diptote — not a naṣb. The wrong rule mispredicts on a definite/iḍāfa diptote (في القرآنِ) which reverts to kasra.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «زَيْدًا» فِي «هَذَا ضَارِبٌ زَيْدًا».
question_en: Parse زَيْدًا in «هَذَا ضَارِبٌ زَيْدًا».
expected_answer: مفعول به منصوب لاسم الفاعل «ضارب» — mafʿūl bih of the ism fāʿil ḍārib (which, being munawwan and of present sense, works like its verb).
expected_reasoning: An ism fāʿil that is munawwan (or has ال) and denotes ḥāl/istiqbāl 'amal's like its verb and takes a mafʿūl bih → زيدًا manṣūb.
wrong_reasoning_trap: زيدًا is manṣūb as a tamyīz clarifying ضارب.
why_trap_wrong: Right ending, wrong path: زيد is the patient of the striking — a mafʿūl bih — not a tamyīz (which would be jāmid and answer 'in what respect'). The 'amal of the ism fāʿil is the whole point; tamyīz misanalysis breaks on the definite case (الضاربُ زيدًا).

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «الرَّجُلُ» فِي «نِعْمَ الرَّجُلُ زَيْدٌ».
question_en: Parse الرَّجُلُ in «نِعْمَ الرَّجُلُ زَيْدٌ».
expected_answer: فاعل مرفوع لـ«نِعْمَ» — marfūʿ fāʿil of the praise-verb niʿma (and زيد is the makhṣūṣ bi-l-madḥ).
expected_reasoning: niʿma is a jāmid past verb of praise; its fāʿil (here the ال-definite الرجل) is marfūʿ, and the praised noun زيد is marfūʿ as makhṣūṣ (mubtadaʾ/khabar).
wrong_reasoning_trap: الرجلُ is a mubtadaʾ marfūʿ and نعم is its khabar muqaddam.
why_trap_wrong: Right ending, wrong path: نعم is a FIʿL (jāmid verb of praise), not a noun; الرجل is its fāʿil, and the construction is a jumla fiʿliyya of praise with a special makhṣūṣ — not a mubtadaʾ/khabar nominal sentence.

question_ar: أَعْرِبْ «قَائِمًا» فِي «كَانَ زَيْدٌ قَائِمًا».
question_en: Parse قَائِمًا in «كَانَ زَيْدٌ قَائِمًا».
expected_answer: خبر كان منصوب — khabar kāna, manṣūb.
expected_reasoning: kāna nāqiṣa raises its ism (زيد marfūʿ) and naṣb's its khabar (قائمًا) — the predicate that completes the deficient verb.
wrong_reasoning_trap: قائمًا is a ḥāl manṣūb from زيد.
why_trap_wrong: Right ending, wrong path: kāna nāqiṣa REQUIRES a khabar; قائمًا is that khabar, not a ḥāl. The ḥāl reading leaves kāna with no predicate and changes 'Zayd was standing' into 'Zayd existed while standing'.
